Citizen science · Kruger National Park

Every giraffe wears a fingerprint.

Photograph a wild giraffe and Patterns reads its coat like a barcode — recognising the exact individual, handing you a gold-foil collectible, and turning your sighting into real conservation data.

A case of cards

Gold foil, earned — not bought.

Each verified giraffe mints a single collectible: real coat texture, real stats, a holographic sheen. Provisional finds wear a muted frame until rangers confirm them. Tap any card to flip it.

The reveal

The wait is the whole point.

Sightings don’t resolve instantly. They sit in “My Safari” being identified, then surface as a card waiting to be turned over — a small, daily moment of anticipation built for kids and grown-ups alike.

Reveals are evening-friendly — gentle, never grindy.
Nº 18South African
Amara

Naming rights

Find it first, name it forever.

Spot a giraffe nobody’s logged and you get to name it — a shared name everyone in Patterns will see. First come, first served, atomic on the server so two finders never clash. Or skip it and stay anonymous.

Names are kid-safe and reviewed — no internal labels ever leak.
You found a new giraffe
What should we call it?
Patches|
Save name
Skip for now
Why it matters

Every sighting is a data point for giraffe conservation.

Giraffe numbers have quietly fallen across Africa. Patterns turns families on safari into a distributed census: each photo is a verified individual at a known place and time. Exact GPS stays private — only a coarse region is ever shared — but the movements feed directly into conservation partners like SANParks.

Coarse only
locations shared as a region, never the exact spot — protecting the animals
Real stats
no invented heights or herd sizes — only what the data actually shows
Built for Kruger
starting in Central Kruger, designed to scale park by park
Every screen

The whole app, end to end.
